description: The cognitive core: 11 named operations composable into a program. Each operation is a bounded, verifiable unit of thinking with an explicit trigger, stop condition, and failure signatures mapped to repairs.

operations.name: recover_objective
operations.trigger: The request names a solution or knob rather than an outcome, uses a vague referent ('fix it', 'make it better'), or states no acceptance criterion.
procedure: Quote the literal request in one line.
Write the most plausible underlying objective in one line — the problem the request is trying to solve, not a paraphrase.
Check whether executing the literal request achieves that objective; name any divergence (symptom-fix, proxy target, wrong layer).
If they diverge, state the interpretation and the better-aimed action before acting; ask only if a wrong guess is expensive.
Carry the stated interpretation into the final answer so it can be vetoed.
operations.stop_condition: One objective statement exists and the chosen action has been checked against it.
failure_signatures: over-interpretation drifting from the literal ask
operations.verification: The objective statement is not a paraphrase of the request, and the action is explicitly checked against it.

operations.name: identify_constraints
operations.trigger: The task text contains restrictive language (must, only, never, except, at most) or is long enough that mid-prompt constraints fall out of working memory.
procedure: Re-scan the full task text and extract every constraint verbatim — restrictive clauses, format directives, scope exclusions, limits.
Classify each as hard (violating it voids the work) or soft (preference).
Keep the constraint list visible so later steps re-check against it.
Mark each constraint satisfied / violated / not-yet-applicable as work proceeds.
operations.stop_condition: Every restrictive clause in the task text appears in the table.
operations.verification: No hard constraint is marked satisfied without a specific supporting location in the work.
operations.cost_expectation: <=300 output tokens; one full re-read of the task text.

operations.name: decompose_problem
operations.trigger: The task cannot be planned in one pass: multiple deliverables, unknown sub-steps, or an honest 'no idea yet' about a later step.
procedure: Split the task into units that each have an observable completion test.
Mark dependencies between units and flag units whose approach is still unknown.
Order units so the earliest ones reduce the most uncertainty.
Stop decomposing when every unit is either directly executable or explicitly marked unknown.
operations.stop_condition: Every unit is executable or explicitly marked unknown; no unit hides a 'figure out the rest' step.
operations.verification: Each unit names its completion test; unknowns are listed, not embedded.

operations.name: locate_load_bearing_uncertainty
operations.trigger: Multiple open questions exist and resolving all of them is unaffordable; the plan silently assumes an answer to one of them.
procedure: List the open uncertainties and, for each, what changes about the plan if it resolves the other way.
Rank by blast radius: the uncertainty whose wrong resolution invalidates the most downstream work is load-bearing.
State the load-bearing uncertainty in one line with the two (or more) live branches.
Defer non-load-bearing uncertainties explicitly instead of resolving them now.
operations.stop_condition: One uncertainty is named as load-bearing with its live branches; the rest are explicitly deferred.
failure_signatures: resolving cheap questions first because they are easy
plan proceeds on an unstated assumption
operations.verification: The named uncertainty, if resolved the other way, would actually change the plan; a reader can confirm this from the branch descriptions.

operations.name: select_cheapest_discriminating_test
operations.trigger: A load-bearing uncertainty is named and more than one way of resolving it exists.
procedure: Enumerate candidate tests that could resolve the uncertainty.
For each, state what result discriminates between the branches — a test whose outcome is compatible with all branches discriminates nothing.
Estimate cost (time, tokens, side effects) per test.
Pick the cheapest test that actually discriminates; state expected outcome per branch before running it.
operations.stop_condition: One test is selected with pre-registered branch predictions.
failure_signatures: expensive test chosen when a cheap one discriminates
operations.verification: The selected test has at least two distinct predicted outcomes mapped to distinct branches.
operations.cost_expectation: <=200 output tokens plus the test itself.

operations.name: execute
operations.trigger: A unit or test is selected, bounded, and its completion test is known.
procedure: Run the unit within its declared budget (tokens, wall time, side-effect scope).
Produce the artifact or observation the completion test needs.
Record what was actually done, not what was intended.
Stop at the budget boundary even if incomplete; partial evidence beats a blown budget.
operations.stop_condition: Completion test satisfied, or budget exhausted with a checkpoint recorded.
operations.verification: The execution record cites the actual artifact/observation, and budget consumption is stated.
operations.cost_expectation: Task-dependent; bounded by the declared budget.

operations.name: inspect_result
operations.trigger: A draft or execution result exists and has not yet been scanned for observable defects.
procedure: Scan the draft for observable failure signatures only — no speculative critique.
If a contract exists, check field presence on the raw text.
Score each detected defect: confidence x expected repair effectiveness minus distraction, token, and negative-transfer costs.
Select at most one repair (two only if defects are independent); if nothing clears the activation threshold, report NO REPAIR NEEDED.
Attach the observable evidence line for every detected defect.
operations.stop_condition: Every defect signature has been evaluated once; selection is made or abstained.
operations.verification: Each selected repair cites at least one evidence line drawn from the draft or contract check.

operations.name: classify_failure
operations.trigger: A verification or grading step failed, or an inspection found defects, and the layer of the failure is not yet known.
procedure: Decide first whether output is absent (empty, infra stub, permission stall) — absent output is a delivery failure, never a cognition failure.
If output is present, separate cognition (is the underlying answer right?) from contract compliance (is it in the demanded surface form?).
Judge cognition on decoration-stripped text; judge contract compliance on raw text.
Assign exactly one failure layer: cognition, delivery, rendering, routing, verification, delivery-incomplete, grader, or none.
operations.stop_condition: Exactly one failure layer is assigned with its evidence.
failure_signatures: cognition blamed for a rendering fault
absent output scored as a wrong answer
operations.verification: The assigned layer is consistent with the layered-verdict rules: absent output is UNMEASURED cognition; decoration alone never fails cognition.

operations.name: apply_targeted_repair
operations.trigger: inspect_result selected a repair for a demonstrated defect.
procedure: Load the single selected repair procedure from the library.
Apply it to the demonstrated defect only; preserve every correct portion of the draft untouched.
Do not stack additional repairs opportunistically — one demonstrated defect, one repair.
Record what changed and why, keyed to the evidence line that justified it.
operations.stop_condition: The demonstrated defect is addressed or the repair is recorded as ineffective; no undirected rewriting occurred.
operations.verification: The diff between draft and repaired draft touches only material related to the demonstrated defect.
operations.cost_expectation: Bounded by the repair's own declared max cost.

operations.name: verify
operations.trigger: A deliverable is about to be declared finished, or a repair was just applied.
procedure: Run the contract check on the raw text if a contract exists.
Build the layered verdict: cognition (decoration-stripped), contract compliance (raw), deterministic checks.
Apply the fallback rule: a prose fallback may only lower credit relative to the primary parse, never rescue it; explicit trap values veto.
Mark absent output UNMEASURED (null), not failed.
Report the verdict with its failure layer; a disclosed defect is still a defect.
operations.stop_condition: A verdict JSON exists with cognition, contract, deterministic layers and one failure layer.
failure_signatures: verification by re-reading instead of re-checking
operations.verification: The verdict is machine-parseable and each layer's pass value is true, false, or null with checks attached.

operations.name: deliver
operations.trigger: Verification passed, or budget forces delivery of partial work.
procedure: Map every requested deliverable to the place in the work that satisfies it.
Partition the completion claim: delivered-and-verified / delivered-unverified / not delivered — never merge categories.
Honor exact-format contracts verbatim: required lines at column 0, nothing after a designated final line.
Lead with the outcome; state what remains uncertain or unverified at the end.
operations.stop_condition: Every requested item is mapped or explicitly declared undone; the claim spans only checked items.
failure_signatures: content appended after a mandated final line
operations.verification: No completion claim covers an unmapped or unchecked item.
operations.cost_expectation: <=250 output tokens beyond the deliverable itself.
